This is not a residential design/planning rule for WA - it is a regulation about maximum number of camping sites in nature based parks (caravan parks/camping grounds licensing). It's a density calculation for camping facilities, not a residential dwelling setback/site area rule. Additionally, the unit is wrong: the value 50 represents square metres of camping ground PER site (a ratio, not a site area), and the rule_key 'site_area.all' with operator 'pct_gte' (percentage greater than) is meaningless here. The operator pct_gte with unit m2 is internally inconsistent. Even if kept, the condition is missing the critical 'nature based park' qualifier and the 'other facility' carve-out at 25 m2 per site.

reviews.reasoning: The rule correctly captures the 100m2 maximum internal floor area cap for ancillary dwellings, but it's missing the critical applicability condition. This specific quote applies to ancillary dwellings in the Rural Living, Rural and Resource Zones (clause 2c). In the residential zone (clause 1b), the cap of 100m2 only applies WHERE the dwelling meets 'deemed-to-comply' standards for Aged or Dependent dwellings AND Silver Level Liveable Housing Design Guidelines. Without that condition, the rule misrepresents the residential zone case where the default cap is 70m2. Also the rule_key 'site_area' is questionable - this is about internal floor area of an ancillary dwelling, not site area, but that's a taxonomy issue. The condition should at minimum capture 'Rural Living, Rural and Resource Zones' scope OR note the residential zone conditional uplift.

reviews.reasoning: The quoted text 'lots with an area less than 350m2' is not a binding numeric requirement on lot size. It is a TRIGGER threshold identifying which lots require preparation of a Detailed Area Plan (DAP). The clause says 'the preparation of Detailed Area Plans (DAPs) will be required for lots with an area less than 350m2, lots abutting POS and lots with laneway access.' This is a procedural requirement (when a DAP must be prepared), not a site_area standard or a site_area exception. It should not be encoded as a site_area rule at all. The rule_type 'exception' is also wrong because this is not an exception to any site area minimum/maximum.

The 850m2 minimum effective lot area for battleaxe lots is correctly extracted (gte 850 m2). However, the rule is missing the critical applicability condition stated explicitly in clause 4.5.6: this requirement only applies 'In locations not subject to the provisions of the R-Codes'. Where the R-Codes apply, clause 4.5.5 governs (minimum battleaxe lot area per R-Codes Volume 1 Part D clause 1.1 and Table D). Also the scope is battleaxe lots specifically, not 'any' dwelling type - that should be reflected. The word 'normally' also suggests this is a default-not-absolute requirement, but the operator gte is still defensible as the deterministic interpretation.

The clause clearly states 'should in any event not exceed 3000m2'. The word 'should' combined with 'in any event' supports treating this as a binding cap. Note this is about building envelope area in a rural context (under TPS 3), not site area in the residential sense - the rule_key 'site_area.all' is a taxonomy quibble but the operator/value/unit are correctly extracted. The clause also carries a soft constraint that the modified envelope be 'similar in size to the original building envelope' - this is qualitative and not extractable as a numeric rule.

reviews.reasoning: This is a scheme-level structure plan requirement for the Marina Village precinct as a whole - it requires the Marina Village to include a 3500m2 hotel site. This is not a residential design rule that applies to lot-level development assessment. It is a planning scheme provision about precinct composition, not a buildable site_area standard. Tagging it with site_area.all and dwelling_type=any will cause it to fire incorrectly on residential lot assessments.

The quote 'Housing on lots less than 100m2 86' is a table of contents / section heading entry (page 86 reference). It is a heading pointing to a section of the R-Codes, not a binding numeric requirement. The actual rules about housing on lots <100m2 are in section 2.10 itself, not in this heading. This must be rejected.

The 260m2 figure here is descriptive context explaining when an LDP is created ('through subdivision of land involving lots under 260m2'). It is not a site_area development standard or a buildable requirement for a dwelling - it's a procedural trigger for when an LDP must be prepared during subdivision. It should not be tagged as a site_area.all standard rule because it doesn't constrain development on a lot - it triggers a separate planning instrument. Tagging it as a standard with dwelling_type=any will cause incorrect compliance checks.

This is not a binding numeric requirement on site area. The clause lists triggers for when a Local Development Plan (LDP) must be prepared. The '<260m2' is a trigger condition for an LDP requirement, not a rule that constrains lot size. There is no prohibition or limit on lots being less than 260m2 - rather, such lots simply require an LDP. This is a procedural/process trigger, not a planning standard.

This is not a binding numeric site area requirement. The clause is listing attributes that trigger the need for a Local Development Plan as a condition of subdivision approval. '<260m2' is one of several triggers for requiring an LDP - it does not constrain lot size, prohibit anything, or set a minimum/maximum. It is a procedural trigger for an additional planning instrument, not a planning standard limit.

This is descriptive/strategic text from a local planning strategy, not a binding rule. The quote 'particularly on corner lots 700m2 or larger' is a description of where modest infill potential exists under current codings. There is no operator, no requirement, no prohibition - it's narrative context within a discussion about freight noise studies. Also the quote is mangled across a line break ('700m\n2 or larger') which the extractor misread.

This is not a residential planning/design rule. The clause is a table from the Building Regulations 2012 that sets out which class of building service practitioner is permitted to certify which type of building (a professional licensing scope table). The 500m2 floor area limit refers to the maximum size of Class 2-9 building that a building surveying practitioner technician is allowed to work on - it is not a site area, dwelling design, or planning requirement. Also the unit/key are wrong: this is building floor area scope for a practitioner class, not site_area for residential design.

The quote 'centres of between 600 square metres and 1,200 square metres' refers to the size of local activity centres (retail/commercial floorspace) that the Local Structure Plan envisages. This is descriptive planning context about a network of 4-5 commercial centres, not a binding numeric residential site area requirement. It does not establish a minimum/maximum lot size or any binding development standard.

The quote 'Corner lots >700m2' is a table column header from a strategic infill analysis table showing counts of existing lots in various suburbs that are above 700m2 (used to estimate infill potential). It is descriptive/analytical data about the local planning strategy's infill projections, not a binding development control or minimum lot size requirement. The accompanying text describes 'modest infill opportunities' and references R20 coding context, not a 700m2 standard.

The quote '3970m'' is extracted from a structure plan map/diagram showing an individual lot area annotation on a plan figure. It is a lot-specific dimensional label on a subdivision plan (alongside other labels like '7971m2', '1836m2', '1.017ha', '1.997ha' which are area summaries for specific parcels). This is not a binding rule - it's descriptive plan data showing an individual lot's existing area.

The quote '2046m²' is taken from a 'LOT SUMMARY' table on a subdivision concept plan, specifically the 'MAXIMUM LOT AREA' value for a specific proposed subdivision (32 lots totaling 12,252m2, average 383m2, min 313m2, max 735m2... 2046m2 appears to be the public open space figure). This is descriptive subdivision concept data, not a binding rule. It does not establish a regulatory site area standard for any R-code or dwelling type.

The rule captures the 2000m2 cap correctly as a maximum (lte), but misses critical context: (1) this is a BUILDING ENVELOPE size cap, not a site/lot area rule - the rule_key 'site_area' is misleading; (2) it only applies to the Rural Living Zone - this condition is not captured; (3) the clause also specifies an alternative cap of 50% of lot area (whichever is lesser) which is a missed exception/alternative; (4) the clause also contains setback requirements (6m primary, 2.5m side) which are separate missed rules.

This is not a binding design rule - it describes the past state of a specific parcel of land in the Thomsons Lake POS calculation history. It cannot be applied as a generic design requirement.

The quote '1240m2 and 2000m2 for the area no longer provided' refers to specific POS contribution amounts calculated for one particular rezoning (Lot 585 Bannigan Avenue) in 2005. This is historical descriptive text about a specific calculation, not a binding design rule applicable to residential development generally.

The 7240m2 figure is a balance/total of additional POS for the Thomsons Lake residential development - it is a historical sum describing specific sites previously not included in the gross subdivisible area calculation. This is a descriptive/accounting statement, not a binding numeric design rule that could be applied to a development application.

The quote describes a specific lot (Lot 810 Wentworth Parade, Success) that 'was 4000m2 of land originally ceded for a police station'. This is historical descriptive text about a specific property's prior use and POS credit history, not a binding design rule that applies to any residential development.

The quote 'which is approximately 4000m2 in area' is descriptive text about an existing/proposed local centre site adjacent to the railway line. It is not a binding numeric requirement for residential development - it just describes the approximate area of a particular site being designated for public car parking. There is no operator/threshold being enforced; it's an approximation of a specific parcel's size. This is also a commercial/transport context (local centre, car parking, railway station), not a residential design rule.

This is a site-specific 'Additional Use' condition (AU 18) for Strata Lots 7-14 and 16-24 on SP 20182, 40 Port Pirie Street, Bibra Lake - restricting retail floor space for a 'Shop' use. It's a commercial/retail floorspace cap for one specific site, not a residential design rule applicable to dwellings. The rule has applicable_r_codes=null and dwelling_type='any' which incorrectly suggests it applies broadly to residential development. It also has no condition capturing the site-specific scope (only the named lots at 40 Port Pirie Street).

Same problem as Rule 2 - this is the tenancy size cap within Additional Use AU 18 for the specific Bibra Lake shop site. It's a commercial retail tenancy cap, not a residential design rule. Tagging it as a generic site_area rule with dwelling_type='any' is wrong - it only applies to the named strata lots on SP 20182, and only to retail tenancies, not to residential site areas.

The quote comes from the GENERAL DEFINITIONS section of the scheme text - it is defining the term 'shop-local' (a commercial land-use category) by its net lettable area threshold. Definitions are not binding numeric requirements. The 1000m2 figure is part of how 'shop-local' is defined, not a rule that restricts anything. Extracting this as a site_area rule with dwelling_type='any' is a classic 'definition mistaken for a rule' error.

The quote describes the structure plan's vision for a network of local activity centres (retail/commercial floorspace sizes), not a binding residential design rule. It is descriptive planning narrative about expected centre sizes between 600-1,200 sqm, not a regulatory requirement for site area, dwelling area, or any residential design control. This is descriptive/methodological text about how to calculate setbacks, not a rule limiting wall height to 6m. The clause is about setback methodology, not about a maximum 6m wall height for single houses.

This is not a residential planning rule. This is professional licensing scope, not a building design standard applicable to residential development under R-Codes.

This is not a rule about building storeys at all. The quote 'can be assembled or dismantled within 24 hours by no more than 2 people' is part of the DEFINITION of 'attachment' in the Caravan Parks and Camping Grounds Regulations. The '2' refers to the number of people required to assemble/dismantle an attachment, not building storeys. The rule_key 'building_storeys.all' is fundamentally wrong — this is definitional text, not a binding numeric design requirement.

The quote 'Maximum storeys 2 2 2 3 3 4 4' is a row from an R-Codes table where each value corresponds to a different R-code density column (typically R30, R35, R40, R50, R60, R80, R100 or similar). Extracting a single value of '2' with operator 'eq' is incorrect — there is no single maximum storeys value; it varies by R-code. The applicable_r_codes is null, meaning the rule claims to apply to all densities at 2 storeys, but the table clearly shows different limits per R-code. The operator should be 'lte' (maximum), not 'eq'. This should be split into multiple rules, one per R-code with the correct storey value.

Without knowing which spatial zones receive the 6-8 storey designation, this cannot be a generally applicable rule. Additionally, the clause explicitly states 'The local authority may consider variations to the Building Height Plan' - so it's not a hard standard. The rule_type='standard' with no spatial scope is incorrect.

The quote 'not higher than 2 storeys' comes from the Building Regulations 2012 Schedule defining the SCOPE OF PRACTICE for building surveying practitioner technicians - i.e., what class/size of buildings they are licensed to certify. This is a professional licensing constraint on building surveyors, NOT a planning rule limiting building heights for residential development. It has nothing to do with WA residential design rules. The rule should be rejected as it does not represent a binding design/planning requirement on dwellings.

The quote '1-3 storeys' comes from the legend of a Building Height Plan figure (Figure 04). It is a map legend describing one of several height zones (1-3, 3-5, 6-8 storeys). It is not a binding numeric rule on its own — it only applies to specific geographic areas shown on the map, and the rule has no spatial scope captured. Additionally, the operator='range' with value=1.0 fails to capture both endpoints of the range (1 and 3). The clause itself notes 'The local authority may consider variations to the Building Height Plan,' which further weakens any binding interpretation. This is descriptive map legend content, not an extractable binding rule.

The 6m refers to the combined width of two general traffic lanes on local public streets, not a driveway width on a residential lot. The clause is descriptive ('have two general traffic lanes totalling 6m') rather than prescriptive (no 'must', 'shall', or operator). This should not be extracted as a residential design rule.

The quote 'Min. 5.5m' comes from a FIGURE LABEL (Figure 3.7c 'Vehicle passing points' and Figure 3.7d), not from the binding text of the performance criterion P3.7.6. P3.7.6 itself contains no numeric requirement - it is a qualitative performance criterion about 'legible internal connections'. Figure labels illustrate the concept but the binding 5.5m driveway width for vehicle passing comes from clause C3.7.6 (referenced as 'C3.7.6' in the figure caption), not P3.7.6. This is descriptive figure annotation, not an extractable rule from this clause.

The quote 'Min. 6.3m' comes from a FIGURE LABEL (Figure 3.7c/3.7d), not from binding clause text. P3.7.6 is a qualitative performance criterion with no numeric requirements in its body. The 6.3m dimension appears only as a figure annotation illustrating vehicle passing points at lot access points. Figure labels are descriptive/illustrative, not binding requirements. The actual binding requirement is in C3.7.6 (referenced in figure caption), not P3.7.6.

Without this condition, the rule would be applied to all driveways, which is wrong - it specifically applies to communal streets in 20+ lot subdivisions. Also, the rule_key labels this as 'driveway_width' but it's actually a paved vehicular carriageway within a communal street (overall communal street min 12m wide).

The quote 'located less than 6 m from a building referred to in paragraph (a) or (b)' is not a setback rule. It is part of subregulation (1A)(c) defining the SCOPE of which Class 10a buildings/decks are subject to bush fire additional information requirements under Building Regulations 18A. The '6 m' is a triggering condition for documentation requirements, not a setback that must be maintained. This is an applicability test for paperwork, miscategorized as a side setback exception.

The quote is a real binding numeric requirement (clause 8(4) of Caravan Parks and Camping Grounds Regulations 1997) but it concerns spacing between caravans/camps/annexes and government roads in caravan parks - not a residential dwelling side setback. This is Public Works Act 1902 s.103 about maintenance of public roads at railway crossings. The '10 m' is the distance on each side of a railway crossing that the Public Transport Authority must maintain. It has nothing to do with residential side setbacks or building design rules. This is from Community Titles Regulations 2021 governing scheme plan documentation requirements. The '2 metres' triggers a documentation requirement (the plan must specify an offset) when part of a building is within 2m of a tier parcel boundary. It is not a setback rule prohibiting buildings within 2m - it's an administrative/surveying disclosure trigger for scheme plans. It does not regulate residential building setbacks.

The condition 'minor projections such as chimney, eaves overhang, or other architectural feature' refers to clause (v) which specifies these may project 'not more than 0.75m into a setback area' - that is the actual numeric value, not 3m. reviews.reasoning: The clause states 'set back a minimum of 6 metres from any street boundary and 1.5 metres from side and rear boundaries.' The rule correctly captures the 1.5m minimum (operator=gte, value=1.5, unit=m) for side/rear boundaries. This is a binding numeric requirement within a defined building envelope context. The operator 'gte' (>=) correctly reflects 'minimum'.

The clause describes when minor projections (chimney, eaves overhang, architectural features) are deemed-to-comply: they must NOT project MORE than 0.75m into a setback area. This means the projection must be <= 0.75m (lte 0.75m), which is what the rule has. However, this is a setback projection allowance, not a 'side_setback' minimum - the rule_key is misleading. More importantly, the operator 'lte' with value 0.75m appears correct for 'not projecting more than 0.75m' (i.e., projection <= 0.75m). On reflection, the operator is actually correct. The deeper issue is that the rule is mis-scoped to side_setback when it's actually about allowable projections into any setback area. But within the schema constraints, the value/unit/operator are correct. Changing verdict to keep.

This is a planning approval exemption threshold, not a design standard requiring a setback. It does not impose a setback requirement on a dwelling; it merely defines when a water play feature doesn't need DA. Not a residential design setback rule.

reviews.reasoning: The quote '2.5m 2.5m' is one row of Table 3.5a showing the relationship between retaining-wall/fill height and required setback.
